fruit.tip: Less sun means grapes retain malic acid and develop underripe aroma precursors: citrus peel, green apple, gooseberry.

body.tip: Less sugar means less alcohol means lighter body. High acidity also makes wines feel leaner. Think Mosel Riesling.

acidity.tip: The clearest climate signal. Grapes accumulate malic acid early; warmth burns it off. Cool climates don't have enough heat, so the malic acid stays.

alcohol.tip: Alcohol comes from sugar, sugar from photosynthesis. Less warmth = less sugar = less alcohol.

fruit.tip: Tart red berries rather than sweet dark ones. The aromatic precursors for dark fruit require more heat to form.

body.tip: Low alcohol and high acid create a lean, bright palate. Cool-climate reds like Beaujolais and Burgundy are meant to be refreshing.

acidity.tip: Same principle as whites: preserved malic acid. Cool-climate Pinot Noir has a brightness that warm-climate reds lack.

alcohol.tip: Thin-skinned cool-climate grapes like Pinot Noir and Gamay rarely exceed 13% ABV.

moderate.label: Moderate

fruit.tip: More warmth means fuller esters. Whites shift from citrus to peach, apricot, melon. Ripe but not cooked.

body.tip: Balanced sugar means enough alcohol for palate weight without heaviness. Moderate acid and alcohol create balance.

acidity.tip: Enough warmth to reduce harsh malic acid while retaining freshness. The balance point is why moderate wines are often the most food-friendly.

alcohol.tip: WSET's medium to medium-high band. Enough weight to feel satisfying without heat.

fruit.tip: Red and dark fruit mix: plum, blackberry, black cherry. Ripe but not overripe.

body.tip: The interplay of moderate alcohol and tannin creates structure. Think Bordeaux: concentrated but balanced.

acidity.tip: Enough acid for food pairing and aging potential, without the sharp bite of cool-climate reds.

alcohol.tip: Upper moderate. Oak-aged reds from Bordeaux, Rioja, and Tuscany typically land here.

moderate.regionNote: 38-47 N or 30-38 S, often moderated by ocean, altitude, or fog

warm.label: Warm

fruit.tip: Intense heat pushes grapes to full ripeness and beyond. Tropical aromas (mango, pineapple) in whites, jammy and stewed fruit in reds.

body.tip: High sugar means high alcohol + high glycerol means viscosity and weight. The wine coats your mouth.

acidity.tip: Prolonged heat breaks down malic acid rapidly. Many warm winemakers legally acidify to prevent wines tasting flat.

alcohol.tip: Intense photosynthesis = high sugar = high alcohol. At these levels you taste warmth in the back of your throat.

fruit.tip: 'Jammy' means fruit cooked on the vine. 'Stewed' and 'dried' are even more extreme. These are your clearest warm-climate signal.

body.tip: High alcohol, high extract, high glycerol. Dense, powerful, mouth-coating. Think Barossa Shiraz, Napa Cabernet.

acidity.tip: Heat destroys malic acid. Even with acidification, warm-climate reds feel softer and rounder.

alcohol.tip: The highest for table wine. At 15%+, you feel the alcohol as heat.

warm.regionNote: Below 42 N or above 30 S, low altitude, no maritime cooling

beginner.note: Green apple, lemon zest, high acidity, 11.5% ABV. Steely and mineral.
beginner.whyCorrect: Every indicator points cool: citrus fruit, high acidity (preserved malic acid), low alcohol.
beginner.whyNotModerate: Moderate wines show stone fruit, not citrus, and 12.5-14% ABV.
beginner.whyNotWarm: Warm climates produce tropical fruit, low acidity, and 14%+.

beginner.note: Ripe mango, pineapple, low acidity, 14.5% ABV. Full-bodied.
beginner.whyCorrect: Tropical fruit + low acid + high alcohol: the warm-climate trifecta.
beginner.whyNotCool: Cool climates cannot produce tropical aromas. 14.5% is well above cool range.
beginner.whyNotModerate: Tropical fruit pushes beyond moderate. Moderate whites show peach and melon.

beginner.note: Peach, apricot, medium acidity, 13% ABV. Balanced.
beginner.whyCorrect: Stone fruit with medium acidity and 13% is the moderate signature.
beginner.whyNotCool: Cool climates show citrus, higher acidity, 11-12.5%.
beginner.whyNotWarm: Warm climates push to tropical. Medium acidity is too fresh for warm.

beginner.note: Cranberry, red cherry, high acidity, 12% ABV. Light-bodied.
beginner.whyCorrect: Red fruit, high acidity, low alcohol, light body. Cranberry is a cool-climate hallmark.
beginner.whyNotModerate: Moderate reds show darker fruit and 13-14.5%.

beginner.note: Jammy blackberry, stewed plum, low acidity, 15% ABV. Dense.
beginner.whyCorrect: 'Jammy' = fruit cooked on the vine. Combined with stewed plum, low acid, 15%.
beginner.whyNotCool: Cool climates produce fresh, tart fruit. 15% requires sugar levels cool grapes never reach.
beginner.whyNotModerate: Moderate blackberry is fresh, not jammy. 15% exceeds moderate range.

beginner.note: Blackberry, black cherry, medium acidity, 13.5% ABV. Medium-full.
beginner.whyCorrect: Dark fruit that's ripe but not jammy, with enough acidity for freshness. 13.5% is squarely moderate.
beginner.whyNotCool: Cool climates rarely produce dark fruit. 13.5% is above cool range.
beginner.whyNotWarm: Medium acidity and fresh (not jammy) fruit keep this out of warm.

beginner.note: Lime, grapefruit, razor-sharp acidity, 12% ABV. Bone-dry.
beginner.whyCorrect: Razor-sharp acidity = high retained malic acid = cool. Citrus fruit, low alcohol.
beginner.whyNotModerate: Moderate climates round off acid. Fruit would shift to stone fruit.
beginner.whyNotWarm: Nothing about this wine is consistent with warm climate.

beginner.note: Baked apple, fig, low acidity, 14.5% ABV. Oily.
beginner.whyCorrect: 'Baked' and 'fig' = fruit transformed by heat. Low acidity, high alcohol, oily texture.
beginner.whyNotCool: Cool climates produce fresh apple, not baked.
beginner.whyNotModerate: Fig and baked apple exceed moderate ripeness.

intermediate.note: White peach, honeysuckle, medium-high acidity, 13% ABV.
intermediate.whyCorrect: Peach is the archetypal moderate descriptor. Medium-high acidity with 13%.
intermediate.whyNotCool: Peach requires more warmth than cool provides.
intermediate.whyNotWarm: Medium-high acidity is too fresh for warm.

intermediate.note: Gooseberry, jalapeno, piercing acidity, 12.5% ABV. Herbaceous.
intermediate.whyCorrect: Green/herbaceous notes from methoxypyrazines, compounds that break down in warm weather.
intermediate.whyNotModerate: Moderate climates break down pyrazines. You'd get passionfruit instead.

intermediate.note: Prune, dark chocolate, low acidity, 15.5% ABV. Chewy.
intermediate.whyCorrect: Prune = dried/overripe fruit. 15.5% requires enormous sugar. Only sustained heat produces this.
intermediate.whyNotCool: Every metric is the opposite of cool.
intermediate.whyNotModerate: 15.5% exceeds moderate. Prune is beyond moderate ripeness.

intermediate.note: Red plum, dried herbs, medium acidity, 13.5% ABV. Earthy.
intermediate.whyCorrect: Red plum (ripe, not overripe) with herbal complexity is a moderate signature. Think Chianti Classico.
intermediate.whyNotCool: Cool reds show cranberry, not red plum.
intermediate.whyNotWarm: Medium acidity and fresh (not stewed) plum = moderate.

intermediate.note: Lychee, rose petal, low acidity, 14% ABV. Oily texture.
intermediate.whyCorrect: Low acid + 14% + oily = warm structurally. Caveat: Gewurztraminer shows these aromas in cool Alsace, but read structural cues on the exam.
intermediate.whyNotCool: Structural profile is incompatible with cool.
intermediate.whyNotModerate: Low acid and 14% push past moderate.

intermediate.note: Mineral, wet stone, citrus pith, high acidity, 12% ABV. Austere.
intermediate.whyCorrect: 'Austere' = acidity dominating fruit. Mineral and citrus pith are cool-climate markers.
intermediate.whyNotModerate: Moderate climates produce enough ripeness to prevent austerity.
intermediate.whyNotWarm: Mineral austerity is impossible in warm climates.

intermediate.note: Cassis, cedar, medium acidity, 14% ABV. Firm tannin.
intermediate.whyCorrect: Fresh cassis (not jammy) with firm structure. 14% is upper-moderate. Think Bordeaux.
intermediate.whyNotCool: Cool Cabernet shows green pepper, not cassis.
intermediate.whyNotWarm: Warm Cabernet = cassis jam, softer tannin, 14.5%+.

intermediate.note: Blackcurrant jam, eucalyptus, low acidity, 15% ABV. Plush.
intermediate.whyCorrect: 'Jam' = fruit cooked by heat. Eucalyptus is a warm Australian marker. Low acid + 15%.
intermediate.whyNotCool: Cool produces cassis, not cassis jam.

advanced.note: Green apple, slate, petrol, high acidity, 8% ABV. Off-dry.
advanced.whyCorrect: Mosel Riesling Kabinett. 8% from stopped fermentation. Petrol is a cool-site Riesling marker.

advanced.note: Apricot, almond, medium acidity, 13.5% ABV. Nutty.
advanced.whyCorrect: Apricot = stone fruit (moderate). Nutty character is winemaking (lees aging). Climate markers are squarely moderate.
advanced.whyNotWarm: Medium acidity too fresh for warm.

advanced.note: Tar, roses, dried cherry, high acidity, 14% ABV. Pale, fierce tannin.
advanced.whyCorrect: Nebbiolo (Barolo). High acid + 14% seems contradictory, but Piedmont's long season preserves acid while building alcohol. Some grapes maintain acidity at full ripeness.
advanced.whyNotCool: 14% is above cool range. Dried cherry = moderate.

advanced.note: Fig, raisin, molasses, very low acidity, 15.5% ABV. Syrupy.
advanced.whyCorrect: Fig, raisin, molasses = extreme heat or dried-grape methods. 15.5% + syrupy = enormous sugar.
advanced.whyNotCool: These flavors are chemically impossible in cool climates.
advanced.whyNotModerate: Even the ripest moderate wines don't reach fig/raisin.

advanced.note: Passion fruit, boxwood, medium-high acidity, 13.5% ABV.
advanced.whyCorrect: Passion fruit seems warm but develops in moderate Sauvignon Blanc. Boxwood (pyrazine) is a cooler marker. The mix of tropical and green = moderate sweet spot.
advanced.whyNotCool: Pure cool Sauvignon shows gooseberry, not passion fruit.
advanced.whyNotWarm: Boxwood and medium-high acidity rule out warm.

advanced.note: Brioche, citrus, toasted almond, very high acidity, 12% ABV. Fine mousse.
advanced.whyCorrect: Traditional method sparkling from France's coldest region. Brioche from lees aging. Searing acidity and citrus at just 12%.
advanced.whyNotWarm: Warm sparkling loses the acidity that defines great fizz.

advanced.note: Black olive, leather, garrigue, medium acidity, 14.5% ABV.
advanced.whyCorrect: Garrigue (thyme, rosemary, lavender) is the most specific warm-climate WSET marker. Black olive and leather are warm secondary aromas.

advanced.note: Red cherry, violet, white pepper, medium-high acidity, 13% ABV. Elegant.
advanced.whyCorrect: Cool-moderate Syrah (Northern Rhone). Medium-high acidity + 13% = moderate. Warm enough for ripe fruit, cool enough for complexity.
advanced.whyNotCool: Overall profile too ripe for cool.
advanced.whyNotWarm: Warm Syrah shows black pepper (not white), jammy fruit, higher alcohol.
